entries.intro: The standing stone near {subject_focus_the} started humming three nights ago. Low, steady, like a note from a horn that never stops.
details: You can feel the vibration if you put your hand on the surface. It pulses, almost like a heartbeat.
The moss on the north side died overnight. Just that side. The rest of the stone is unchanged.
At midnight the hum gets louder. Not by much, but enough that you notice.
reactions: The elders say the stones have always been quiet. This is new, and new things from old stones make people nervous.
Some folks have started leaving offerings at the base: bread, coins, flowers. Can't hurt, I suppose.

statCheck.pass: I pressed my ear to the stone at midnight. Underneath the hum, there's a rhythm: three short, one long, repeating. It's not random. It's a pattern.
statCheck.fail: You lean close to the stone and hear the hum, but it all sounds the same to you. Just a low drone with no variation you can detect.

entries.intro: Every dog in {subject_focus_the} started howling at the same moment last night. All of them, at once, facing the same direction.
details: It lasted exactly as long as it takes to count to sixty. Then they all stopped, at the same time, and went back to sleep.
They were facing the old burial mound on the western hill. Every single one.
reactions: My grandmother would have called that a summoning. The dead calling to the living through the animals that guard them.
I don't put stock in superstition. But I'll admit I didn't sleep well after that.

entries.intro: A crow landed on the village well near {subject_focus_the} and hasn't moved in two days. Won't eat, won't drink, just sits there staring south.
details: People have tried shooing it away. It flutters up, circles once, and lands right back in the same spot.
Its eyes are clouded over, like cataracts, but it's not old. Young bird, healthy plumage.
Other crows gather in the trees nearby but won't come closer. They watch the one on the well like they're waiting.
reactions: The washerwomen won't use the well until it leaves. They're hauling water from the stream instead.
I know it's just a bird. But the way it stares south without blinking makes my skin crawl.

statCheck.pass: I followed its gaze due south. There's nothing there but open field, but the grass in that direction has started growing in a spiral pattern. Tight, deliberate spirals.
statCheck.fail: You look south where the crow is staring but see nothing unusual. Fields, trees, sky. Whatever it's watching, you can't see it.

entries.intro: The moon was the wrong color near {subject_focus_the} two nights ago. Not red like a blood moon: green. Faint, but unmistakable.
details: It lasted about an hour, starting just after midnight. Then it faded back to normal like nothing happened.
The air smelled different while it lasted. Sweet, like overripe fruit, but colder than the night air should have been.
reactions: Half the village saw it. The other half says we were dreaming. Hard to argue when the sky looks normal now.
The old records keeper is digging through the archives. She says there might be mention of something similar from centuries ago.

statCheck.pass: The records keeper found it: a passage describing a green moon that appeared before the founding of the old watchtower. The text calls it "the herald's lantern" and says it marked the opening of something.
statCheck.fail: You ask the records keeper if she found anything, but the old texts are faded and written in a dialect that's hard to parse. No clear answers yet.

entries.intro: The fire in the village hearth near {subject_focus_the} burned blue for an entire evening last week. Same wood, same hearth, but the flames were ice-blue from dusk to midnight.
details: The heat was the same. Food cooked fine. But the light it cast made everything look like it was underwater.
When it turned back to orange at midnight, there was a single white stone sitting in the ashes that wasn't there before. Smooth, round, warm.
reactions: The stone is sitting on the mantle now. Nobody wants to touch it and nobody wants to throw it away.
Blue fire means different things depending on who you ask. None of the meanings are comforting.
